top_weaknesses.issue: Lede is a cold stat dump ('64% in 1974. 56% by 2024.') with no human stakes — Maya bounces on the L train; HN reader feels policy-memo opening
top_weaknesses.fix: Open with the $5T number or a one-line image: 'Eighteen points of US national income are quietly migrating from paychecks to compute owners. That's a $5 trillion slice.'

top_weaknesses.issue: Crescendo and absolutist tells trip AI-essay detectors: 'This is the single most important macroeconomic fact', 'It is the prize', 'The arithmetic is not subtle', 'It is larger than X. It is larger than Y.'
top_weaknesses.fix: Cut 'It is the prize' and 'The arithmetic is not subtle'; soften 'single most important' to a less rhetorical claim

top_weaknesses.issue: Speculative 46% projection treated as near-fact; assertions like 'no competitive layer' and 'a few thousand entities' lack sourcing
top_weaknesses.fix: Frame 46% as scenario projection; add a hedge or BEA/BLS baseline citation for the labor-share series; cite one source for the concentration claim

top_weaknesses.issue: Anchor texts are full claim sentences ('MIT research finds automation alone explains 50% to 70%...', 'Hyperscalers spent up to $660 billion...') — spammy and hard to scan
top_weaknesses.fix: Trim to 'MIT: automation drove 50–70% of wage-inequality rise' / 'Big Tech AI capex hit ~$660B in one year'

top_weaknesses.issue: Missing one concrete human-scale example or company-line-item to anchor the macro thunder for non-econ readers
top_weaknesses.fix: Add 2 sentences: a real role displaced + the corresponding shift in firm operating income (e.g., support staff cut → opex line drops → flows to chip vendor)

consensus_summary: Strong closing question and a coherent definition-mechanism-implications spine, but the lede is too cold for mobile and the middle leans crescendo-rhetorical in ways that ping AI-slop. Soften absolutes, ground the 46% projection, trim spammy anchors, and add one concrete example.
